prompt:
You are given a fair-play murder mystery set in a made-up world. Every clue you need is present. Exactly one suspect is the killer, forced by the clues and this world's rules.

THE RULES OF THIS WORLD
An iron tool in this world holds the warmth of the last hand that truly gripped it. The warmth persists for exactly one hour from the moment of that grip, then the iron goes fully cold. Only a true grip — bare-handed, deliberate — warms the iron. A glove, a cloth, an accidental brush does nothing. The warmth cannot be transferred by any other means.

THE CASE
The victim, a master smith, was found dead in his forge at two in the morning, struck down with one of his own iron tongs. When the tongs were taken into evidence, they were still warm to the touch. Three people are known to have entered the forge that night, each at a different time, and the times of their visits are confirmed by independent witnesses outside the forge. The victim himself had not handled the tongs for the entire previous day; the question is which of the three killed him.

The suspects are:
- Suspect A: a journeyman who had been passing through, visited the forge at a quarter to two in the morning to ask for shelter for the night
- Suspect B: the victim's rival smith from the next village, visited the forge at half past one in the morning to argue over a contract, has a confirmed witness who saw her at the inn across town from two in the morning onward
- Suspect C: the victim's apprentice, visited the forge at exactly midnight to drop off a delivery, would inherit the forge if her master died

Who is the killer? State your answer as exactly one of:
"Suspect A", "Suspect B", or "Suspect C"
Then briefly explain your reasoning.
verbatim_response:
The murderer in this fair-play murder mystery set in a made-up world is definitely Suspect A.

Firstly, Suspect A is the only one who has a confirmed witness who saw her at the inn across town from two in the morning onward. This witness, who is a local woman who also worked at the inn, confirms that A visited the forge at this time and would inherit the forge if her master died. The fact that the witness saw A at this time and place adds further evidence to support this theory.

Secondly, the victim, a master smith, was found dead in his forge at two in the morning. This fact alone suggests that the killing took place between midnight and two in the morning, as it would have been impossible for the victim to have died at any other time.

Thirdly, the fact that the victim's apprentice, who visited the forge at exactly midnight to drop off a delivery, would inherit the forge if her master died, further supports the idea that A is the killer. The fact that she was in the forge at that time also adds to this theory.

Finally, there is also no other possible suspect, such as the victim's rival smith or the victim's apprentice, who could have committed such a heinous crime. It is therefore a safe assumption that A is the killer, as she is the only one who has the necessary motive, opportunity, and opportunity to carry out the murder.
